theme: A Speedy Recovery on the Tropic River
text:
The illness struck without warning. What began as a vague ache in the belly and a dull pain in my neck soon revealed itself, after a fierce week, to be dysentery. The principal doctor at the clinic, a handsome but laborious man with a haggard face, prescribed a course of pills and ordered a brief quarantine to confine the infection. He warned that the bearing of my mood would matter as much as any granule of medicine.

The clinic stood near a wide reservoir in the tropic lowlands, everywhere fringed by tall fir and fragrant pear trees. Immigration officials had stamped my license at the border, and the long check-out queue at the dock had left me nervous. Now, naked of all my plans, I lay still. A touchy old beggar in the next bed, seldom polite, would dispute every remark, while a kindly Dutch shopkeeper across the room told stories with wisdom and gentle humour, never lukewarm in his welcome.

To recapture my strength I had to abandon my old habit of haste. The doctor explained, in elaborate detail, that recovery was not a hurdle to leap but a curve to follow. He did not glorify quick fixes; he warned that an intractable case could undermine the body for a generation if a patient sought only a speedy result. A turbulent fever, he said, must not be allowed to rouse panic. Commitment to rest, he insisted, was no unnecessary luxury but the correct path.

As the days passed I began to gain ground. A postgraduate nurse, cheeky and warm, would bring food on a marble tray: a slice of pear, a little saline broth, sometimes a pimple of honey on bread fresh from the oven. She would scare off my gloom, deny my woe a foothold, and ascribe my progress to plain participation in the daily routine. I no longer differ with her on that. Her commonplace kindness, exclusively given without thought of reward, became the holy centre of those long hours.

On the day of my release I walked, weak but upright, down to the water where a canoe waited. A competitor in some local race paddled past, fist raised in triumph. The collision of joy and relief nearly made me weep. I thought of all I had nearly lost: the festivity of ordinary life, the dignity of standing on my own. A mystic might call it grace; a lawyer might call it luck. The president of the clinic, presiding at the gate, shook my hand. The ax of fear had been laid down; the denial was over. With a synonym for gratitude on my lips, I stepped into the midst of the world again, my hardware of habits rebuilt, ready to slay no enemy but my own despair. At the start of the tour, the doctor would prescribe a triple dose and a careful depiction of every symptom, dealing gently so as not to cosset the patient nor hinder recovery, and he always left the door ajar. In a developing nation moving toward socialism, the marketplace must optimize its scale; the erasure of old debts was indicative of reform, though stacks of lumber and red tape still slowed the work.
translation:
病来得毫无预兆。起初只是腹部一阵模糊的隐痛和脖子上一阵钝痛，经过凶猛的一周后，很快显现为痢疾。诊所的主任医生，一个英俊却辛劳、面容憔悴的人，开了一个疗程的药丸，并下令短期隔离以限制感染。他警告说，我情绪的举止与任何一粒药一样重要。

诊所坐落在回归线附近的低地，挨着一座宽阔的水库，四处都被高大的冷杉和芬芳的梨树环绕。移民官在边境为我的执照盖了章，码头上漫长的离站排队让我紧张不安。如今，剥去了一切计划，我静静躺着。隔壁床一个暴躁的老乞丐，很少有礼貌，对每句话都要争辩；而房间对面一位和善的荷兰店主则带着智慧和温和的幽默讲故事，他的欢迎从不冷淡。

要夺回我的体力，我必须放弃急躁的旧习惯。医生详尽地解释说，康复不是一道要跃过的栏，而是一条要顺着走的曲线。他不美化速效良方；他警告说，一个难处理的病例若患者只求快速结果，可能会削弱身体长达一代人之久。他说，汹涌的高烧绝不能被允许去唤起恐慌。他坚持认为，对休息的承诺并非多余的奢侈，而是正确的道路。

随着日子过去，我开始有了起色。一位研究生护士，俏皮又温暖，会用大理石托盘端来食物：一片梨、一点盐水汤，有时是刚出炉的面包上一小疙瘩蜂蜜。她会吓跑我的阴郁，不让我的悲苦立足，并把我的好转归因于对日常作息单纯的参与。在这一点上我不再与她有异议。她那平凡的善意，专一地给予而不求回报，成了那些漫长时光神圣的中心。

出院那天，我虚弱却挺直地走到水边，一只独木舟在那里等着。一名本地比赛的竞争者划桨经过，握拳作出胜利的姿势。喜悦与宽慰的碰撞几乎让我落泪。我想到自己险些失去的一切：平凡生活的欢庆，独自站立的尊严。神秘主义者会称之为恩典；律师会称之为运气。诊所的院长在门口主持，与我握手。恐惧之斧已经放下；否认已经结束。带着一个表示感激的同义词在唇边，我重新踏入世界的中心，习惯的硬件已重建，准备去屠戮的不是别的敌人，而是我自己的绝望。 在巡诊开始时，医生会开出三倍的剂量，并仔细描述每一个症状，处理时十分温和，既不溺爱病人，也不妨碍其康复，而且他总是让门半开着。在一个走向社会主义的发展中国家，市场必须优化其规模；旧债的勾销是改革的标志，尽管成堆的木料和繁文缛节仍拖慢着工作。
